source_paper_title: Tight Running Times for Minimum ℓq-Norm Load Balancing: Beyond Exponential Dependencies on 1/ϵ

Introduction (page 2) and Conclusion (page 46), problem sentence about $m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)$ for $\theta\in(1/2,1]$.

It remains a problem to obtain tight running times when $m = \Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)$ for $\theta \in (1/2, 1]$.
title: Tight PTAS running time for q-norm load balancing with intermediate machines
background_latex:
We consider scheduling on identical parallel machines. There are $m\in\mathbb{Z}_+$ identical machines and $n\in\mathbb{Z}_+$ jobs with processing times $p_j>0$. A schedule is an assignment of jobs to machines; the load of machine $i$ is
\[
C_i := \sum_{j\to i} p_j.
\]
Fix a constant $q>1$. The objective is to minimize the $q$-power sum of loads,
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q,
\]
equivalently the $\ell_q$-norm of the load vector $\big(\sum_i C_i^q\big)^{1/q}$. This problem is denoted $P\|\sum_i C_i^q$.

A polynomial-time approximation scheme (PTAS) for this problem is a family of algorithms parameterized by $\varepsilon>0$ that, on every instance, returns a schedule of objective value at most $(1+\varepsilon)$ times the optimum, with running time of the form $f(1/\varepsilon)\,n^{O(1)}$ (possibly with additional dependence on $m$).

The paper studies fine-grained bounds on the best possible dependence of the PTAS running time on $1/\varepsilon$, as a function of the number of machines $m$. It establishes (under the Exponential Time Hypothesis, ETH) a matching subexponential-in-$1/\varepsilon$ lower bound in the regime $m=\Theta(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})$, and gives faster PTASs in other regimes (e.g., polynomial in $1/\varepsilon$ when $m$ is sufficiently large).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the asymptotically tight (up to polylogarithmic factors) dependence on $1/\varepsilon$ of the fastest PTAS running time for $P\|\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q$ in the regime
\[
m = \Theta\big((1/\varepsilon)^\theta\big) \quad \text{for some fixed } \theta\in(1/2,1].
\]
That is, for each such $\theta$, characterize the smallest achievable function $f(1/\varepsilon, m)$ (in the sense of upper and ETH-based lower bounds) such that there exists a $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation algorithm running in time
\[
f(1/\varepsilon,m) + n^{O(1)}
\]
when $m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)$ and $q>1$ is a fixed constant.

open_problem_quote: It remains a problem to obtain tight running times when $m = \Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)$ for $\theta \in (1/2, 1]$.
subject_classification: Approximation schemes and fine-grained complexity in scheduling
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for asymptotically tight upper/lower bounds as functions of parameters, not a specific inequality that can be refuted by a finite instance. Automated instance search is unlikely to resolve such a complexity-theoretic characterization.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Progress would require new algorithmic ideas or new reductions/ETH-based lower bounds with rigorous proofs; there is no straightforward automatic fitness function that can certify optimal asymptotic running-time dependence for candidate code.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
